Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The current colors match the initial colors. So I'm back at rotation 0 CW. The game is still running, and I've used 17 actions.
Looking at the segmentation, I see:
- Node 12: Y at (17, 10) - fixed yellow block
- Node 13: Y at (17, 16) - fixed yellow block
- Node 20: Y at (19, 42) - rotating yellow block (4x4)
- Node 22: Y at (23, 10) - fixed yellow block
- Node 24: Y at (23, 16) - fixed yellow block
So there are 4 fixed yellow blocks (2x2 each, 4 pixels) and 1 rotating yellow block (4x4, 16 pixels).
Wait, the fixed yellow blocks are 2x2 (4 pixels), and the rotating blocks are 4x4 (16 pixels). This is interesting.
Also, I notice there are charcoal (c) blocks at (19, 16) and (23, 12). These are 2x4 blocks (8 pixels).
Let me look at the layout more carefully:
- Fixed yellow blocks: (17, 10), (17, 16), (23, 10), (23, 16) - 2x2 each
- Charcoal blocks: (19, 16), (23, 12) - 2x4 each
- Rotating blocks: 4x4 each, arranged in a frame
Wait, I also see node 15: c at (19, 16) with 8 pixels, and node 23: c at (23, 12) with 8 pixels. These are charcoal blocks.
But earlier I thought the charcoal blocks were fixed and the rotating blocks were the colored ones. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the rotating blocks are:
- Node 14: w at (19, 12) - 16 pixels (4x4)
- Node 15: c at (19, 16) - 8 pixels (2x4)
- Node 16: g at (19, 18) - 16 pixels (4x4)
- ...
Wait, node 15 is charcoal (c) with 8 pixels. This is not a rotating block (which are 16 pixels). So the charcoal blocks are fixed.
Let me look at the layout:
- Top row of rotating blocks: w (19, 12), c (19, 16), g (19, 18), S (19, 24), b (19, 30), p (19, 36), Y (19, 42), g (19, 48)
Wait, that's 8 blocks in the top row, but I expected 7. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the positions:
- (19, 12): w
- (19, 16): c (charcoal, 8 pixels)
- (19, 18): g
- (19, 24): S
- (19, 30): b
- (19, 36): p
- (19, 42): Y
- (19, 48): g
That's 8 blocks, but the charcoal block is only 8 pixels (2x4), not 16 pixels (4x4). So it's not a rotating block.
Wait, but earlier I thought the rotating blocks were 20 in total. Let me count the 16-pixel nodes:
- Node 14: w (16)
- Node 16: g (16)
- Node 17: S (16)
- Node 18: b (16)
- Node 19: p (16)
- Node 20: Y (16)
- Node 21: g (16)
- Node 25: S (16)
- Node 26: p (16)
- Node 29: p (16)
- Node 30: b (16)
- Node 31: g (16)
- Node 32: S (16)
- Node 33: w (16)
- Node 34: w (16)
- Node 35: b (16)
- Node 36: b (16)
- Node 37: S (16)
- Node 38: p (16)
- Node 39: g (16)
That's 20 nodes with 16 pixels each. These are the rotating blocks.
The charcoal blocks (node 15, 23) have 8 pixels each and are fixed.
So the rotating blocks are 20 in total, arranged in a frame. The charcoal blocks are fixed and separate.
Now, let me think about the goal again. I've been rotating the colors but never completed the level. The game considers the rotation as "no impact" (only HUD changed).
Maybe the goal is not to rotate the colors at all. Maybe the goal is to click on specific rotating blocks to change their color.
Let me try clicking on one of the rotating blocks to see what happens. I'll click on the yellow block at (19, 42).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Try clicking on a rotating block to see if it changes color
# Click on the yellow block at position ~20, 44 (center of 4x4 block at 19,42)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 44}])
print("Result:", result)
print("Board changed:", result.get('board_changed', False))
print("Gameplay changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed', False))

# Check if colors changed
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
positions = [
    (20, 14), (20, 20), (20, 26), (20, 32), (20, 38), (20, 44), (20, 50),
    (27, 50), (33, 50), (39, 50), (45, 50), (45, 44), (45, 38), (45, 32),
    (45, 26), (45, 20), (45, 14), (39, 14), (33, 14), (27, 14),
]
current_colors = [ascii_lines[r][c] for r, c in positions]
print("Current colors:", current_colors)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
